4 min read
Just now

--

I had the idea for months. I had the vision mapped out in my head. What I didn’t have was a reason to stop overthinking and just start. TestSprite’s hackathon gave me that reason.

For a long time, I carried around an idea I genuinely believed in: a platform that brings people’s communities, focus tools, and AI assistance together in one place, instead of scattering them across a dozen disconnected apps. I called it ORBIT.
And for just as long, that idea stayed exactly where most good ideas go to wait — in my head, half-planned, perpetually “not quite the right time yet.”
That changed when I came across TestSprite’s Season 3 Hackathon.
Why a Hackathon Was the Push I Needed
I think a lot of people who’ve sat on an idea will recognize this pattern: it’s not that you lack the vision, it’s that there’s no deadline forcing you to turn vision into something real. Perfectionism and “I’ll start when I know more” can quietly stall a project indefinitely.
A hackathon strips that excuse away. There’s a clock running. There’s a submission that has to exist by a specific date, in a working state, that other people will actually look at. That kind of pressure — the good kind — is exactly what turned ORBIT from an idea I talked about into a live, deployed product with real users interacting with it.
I signed up not fully sure I could pull it off in the time given. I finished with a working platform, a documented testing process, and — more importantly — proof to myself that the idea I’d been protecting in my head could actually survive contact with reality.
What I Actually Built
ORBIT went from a blank repository to a live, publicly accessible platform: real-time community spaces (“Orbits”) with live chat, a content feed, user profiles, and an integrated AI assistant — built and deployed within the hackathon’s timeframe.
That alone would have been a meaningful personal milestone. But the requirement that shaped the experience the most wasn’t the build — it was the testing loop.
The Part That Surprised Me: TestSprite’s CLI
TestSprite’s hackathon didn’t just ask participants to ship something that looked finished. It required actually running the TestSprite CLI against the live, deployed product — creating real automated tests, executing them against the production URL, and documenting what happened when things failed.
That last part is what I didn’t expect to value as much as I did.
Running an automated test against my own live site, I watched it walk through real user flows — signing up, navigating between community spaces, posting content — and then hit a wall: a button that stayed disabled when it shouldn’t have. Not a hypothetical bug. A real one, sitting in production, that I hadn’t caught by eye.
I pulled the failure artifact TestSprite generated, traced it back to the actual state logic controlling that button, fixed it, redeployed, and reran the exact same test. It passed.
That loop — build, test against reality, diagnose, fix, verify — is a genuinely different discipline than “it looks like it works, ship it.” It’s the difference between assuming your product works and knowing it does, because you watched an automated agent prove it under real conditions.
Why This Mattered Beyond the Hackathon Itself
I’ll be honest about something: my project still has rough edges. Building fast under a deadline means some things get deprioritized, and I’ve been transparent with early testers about what’s still being refined. That’s part of the process, not something to hide.
But the testing discipline TestSprite’s format required me to adopt didn’t stop being useful once the hackathon deadline passed. It became part of how I now approach every feature I add: ship it, then actually verify it against the live product instead of trusting that it probably works.
That habit alone — separate from anything about winning or ranking in the competition — was worth participating for.
The Real Takeaway
If you have an idea you’ve been sitting on, waiting for the “right moment” to start, my honest advice is: stop waiting for the moment and go find a deadline instead. A hackathon, a launch date you commit to publicly, a demo you promise to show someone — anything that removes the option of indefinite preparation.
I didn’t build ORBIT because I finally knew enough. I built it because a clock was running and I decided to trust the process of figuring things out as real problems appeared — which, thanks to a structured testing requirement, they did, visibly and fixably, instead of silently breaking for actual users later.

ORBIT is live now, built and tested as part of TestSprite’s Season 3 Hackathon. I’ll be sharing more of the technical build process — architecture decisions, real production bugs, and lessons learned — in an upcoming article series. If you’re curious about the platform or the build process, I’d love to hear your thoughts.
#BuildInPublic #Hackathon #TestSprite #StartupJourney #AITools
